coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots preserve strong blue emission in both aqueous and solid states and their application in intracellular temperature sensing and white light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Junlu
Qin, Liying
Wang, Deyin
coredata.dc:description: The emission of carbon dots is usually quenched in the solid state, thus limiting their application. Herein, we reported the structure and optical properties of carbon dots that preserve intense blue emission in both aqueous and solid states. Structural analysis indicates the obtained carbon dots are largely functionalized by sodium ion (Na+), which could keep the carbon core from direct contact and inhibit the fluorescence resonance energy transfer, leading the carbon dots still emit even after drying to solid powder. Moreover, in addition to the emission intensity, the luminescence lifetime of the aqueous carbon dots responds linearly to temperature in the range from 20 to 70 °C, and is stable at different pH and salinity environments, making them promising optical nanothermometers for probing intracellular temperature via dual mode (emission intensity/lifetime). The applicability of the aqueous carbon dots for intracellular temperature sensing has been demonstrated in living cell studies, while that for the solid carbon dots has been demonstrated by constructing a prototype white LEDs with high color rendering index of 96.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Intracellular thermal sensing
Nanothermometers
White LEDs
Luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) (inset: size distribution histogram of the CDs) and HRTEM (b) of the CDs. The enlarged insets in (b) marked by orderly arranged hexagon show the sp2 domains of the CDs.
FTIR spectra of the obtained CDs and the starting materials.
(a) Full XPS survey scan of the obtained CDs. (b–d) High resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s.

Excitation (λem = 450 nm), UV–visible absorption and emission spectra of the aqueous CDs (5 mg/mL) and solid CDs under different excitations. Insets are the photographs of the aqueous CDs and solid CDs under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation.
Luminescent decay curves of the solid CDs and aqueous CDs under 365 nm pulse excitation (λem = 450 nm).

(a) The emission spectra of the aqueous CDs in the temperature range 20–70 °C; (b) (b) I/I0−T plots of the aqueous CDs upon heating and cooling; (c) Reversible emission of the aqueous CDs during five consecutive heating-cooling cycles between 20 and 70 °C.
(a) Temperature dependent luminescence decay curves of the aqueous CDs; (b) Lifetime of the aqueous CDs plotted as a function of temperature in the range of 20–70 °C.

(a–c) Emission intensity variation of the aqueous CDs as a function of different NaCl concentrations, different pH values and irradiation time; (d) CCK 8 based cytotoxicity assay of TCA-8113 cells after 24 h incubation with different concentrations of CDs.

(a) Confocal laser microscopic image of TCA-8113 cells incubated with CDs in bright field mode. (b–c) Confocal laser microscopic images of TCA-8113 cells after the cellular uptake of CDs in fluorescence mode upon 405 nm light excitation at 20 °C and 37 °C, respectively.

(a) EL spectrum of the fabricated white LEDs driven by 20 mA current (Insets are the images of the unlighted and lighted white LEDs); (b) EL spectra of the white LEDs operated with different forward-bias currents (Insets are the normalized EL spectra).
Nanothermometer based on the temperature-dependent photoluminescence intensity of CDs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a)
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated by using quinine sulfate in H2SO4 solution (0.10 mol/L; φ = 0.54) as standard.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoelectric parameters (CCT, CRI, luminescence efficiency (η) and color coordinates) of the white LEDs lamp as function of operating currents.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots preserve strong blue emission in both aqueous and solid states and their application in intracellular temperature sensing and white light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Stomotology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Stomotology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emission of carbon dots is usually quenched in the solid state, thus limiting their application. Herein, we reported the structure and optical properties of carbon dots that preserve intense blue emission in both aqueous and solid states. Structural analysis indicates the obtained carbon dots are largely functionalized by sodium ion (Na+), which could keep the carbon core from direct contact and inhibit the fluorescence resonance energy transfer, leading the carbon dots still emit even after drying to solid powder. Moreover, in addition to the emission intensity, the luminescence lifetime of the aqueous carbon dots responds linearly to temperature in the range from 20 to 70 °C, and is stable at different pH and salinity environments, making them promising optical nanothermometers for probing intracellular temperature via dual mode (emission intensity/lifetime). The applicability of the aqueous carbon dots for intracellular temperature sensing has been demonstrated in living cell studies, while that for the solid carbon dots has been demonstrated by constructing a prototype white LEDs with high color rendering index of 96.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention due to their high photostability, low toxicity, and superior optical performance, etc [1–6]. To date, great achievements have been made in improving their efficiency, tuning their emission to longer wavelengths, and extending their applications [7–14]. However, when CDs are dried into the solid-state, they always suffer from strong luminescence quenching caused by aggregation due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [15–17]. The severe luminescence quenching greatly limits their applications in white LEDs, latent fingerprints, and anti-counterfeiting [17–25]. As a consequence, different strategies have been proposed to effectively solve this problem, and the common route is to disperse aqueous CDs in polymers (PMMA, PVA, PVP) or to embed them in solid matrices (starch, salt, or silica, etc.) [19–25]. However, the loading fraction of CDs in these methods is usually low, which is not beneficial to achieving strong PL emission output [22,26,27], resulting in solid-state CDs obtained in this way are being unsuitable for use in white LEDs and other solid-state light-emitting fields. Therefore, it is important to develop new strategies to obtain solid-state luminescent CDs. One new strategy is to make the resulting CDs themselves with fluorescence quenching–resistant properties via controlling their structure and surface state. In our previous study, we found that surface functionalization by Na+ can be an effective way for CDs to achieve solid-state luminescence [3,28], thus one of the aims of this work is to further investigate the universality of this strategy. On the other hand, precise sensing of intracellular temperature is important for fundamental cellular activity studies and the early detection of disease [29–31]. For example, it is well recognized that cellular activities, such as cell division, gene expression, enzymatic reactions, metabolism, and pathological states, can be reflected and influenced by changes in intracellular temperature, and that the temperature of cancer cells is higher than that of normal cells due to increased metabolic activity [31]. However, high-precision intracellular temperature measurements are not easy due to the complexity of the intracellular environment [29–31]. Up to now, several nano thermometers based on luminescence thermometry have been reported, including fluorescent polymers, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and lanthanide complex, but they still have problems of low biocompatibility, complicated preparation procedures, and water-insoluble, etc [29–31]. CDs are featured by low toxicity, easy preparation, and good biocompatibility, making them ideal for intracellular temperature sensing. Indeed, recent studies on the temperature-dependent photoluminescence (PL) intensity of CDs have shown that they can be sensors for temperature measurement within living cells [32–36]. Compared with PL intensity, PL lifetime is intrinsically referenced and less affected by the excitation source, also can reduce the distortions caused by complex tissues, which makes the lifetime based temperature measurement method more reliable and practical than the PL intensity based method. However, after reviewing the existing literature, we found few reports and studies that exploit the temperature dependence of the PL lifetime of CD for intracellular temperature sensing.

In this work, aspired by our previous success, we have synthesized self-quenching resistant CDs in solid state, which preserve intense blue emission as that in aqueous state. More importantly, in addition to the emission intensity, the lifetime of the aqueous CDs shows a linear response to temperature in the temperature range from 20 to 70 °C and is stable under different pH and ionic strength conditions, making them promising PL intensity and lifetime-based nano thermometers. The potential applicability of the aqueous CDs and solid CDs has been demonstrated in intracellular imaging and temperature sensing as well as in the construction of prototype white light LEDs.
Details of this section including chemicals, instrumentation and experiments are provided in the ESI†.

Morphology and composition of the obtained CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the obtained CDs are well dispersed without apparent aggregation, and their average size is determined to be about 2.4 nm by statistical analysis the the nearly 40 particles observed in the TEM image (see inset in Fig. 1a). In the meantime, a clear lattice fringe is observed in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b), indicating the crystalline nature of these CDs. The spacing of the lattice fringe is about 0.21 nm, which is very close to the (100) diffraction facet of graphite, suggesting the CDs may have a graphite-liked structure [16,28]. Furthermore, fine and orderly arranged hexagons as marked by yellow circles can be seen in the HRTEM as well (Fig. 1b), which implies that the CDs consist of intact sp2 clusters [37]. The blurred boundary may be caused amorphous surface composed of oxygenic groups. The XRD of the obtained CDs consists of a broad band centred around 32° (Fig. S1, ESI†), which is attributed to the disordered surface of the CDs. The HRTEM and XRD results indicate that the structure of the CDs is composed of a conjugated crystalline core and an amorphous surface.

The structure and composition of the obtained CDs were further characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2 is the FTIR spectra of the obtained CDs, and that of the precursors are also measured for comparison. The FTIR spectrum of the CDs is much similar to that of precursor sodium citrate: the intense absorption band in the 3200–3700 cm−1 region is assigned to the vibration of –OH, and the strong absorption around 1600 cm−1 is assigned to the asymmetric stretch vibrations of carboxylate group (COO-), while that around 1400 cm−1 is assigned to the stretch vibrations of carboxylate group (COO-) [38]. The above FTIR results also manifest the structure of the obtained CDs containing the fragments of sodium citrate.

The XPS spectrum shows (Fig. 3 a) that the obtained CDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and sodium with a trace amount of nitrogen. The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks at 287.9 eV, 285.9 eV and 284.6 eV, which are attributed to C in the forms of CO, C–O and C–C, respectively [19,33]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3c) show that nitrogen is present as graphitic nitrogen (400.5 eV) and pyrroline nitrogen (399.4 eV) [17,39]. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two peaks at 531.8 and 530.8 eV, corresponding to C–O and CO, respectively. The FTIR and XPS spectra indicate that Na+ ions are functionalized on the surface of the carbon cores by forming coordination bonds with the carboxylate group. Combining the results from TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS, it can be concluded that the as-prepared CDs consist of a crystalline graphitic core surrounded by plenty of functional groups including hydroxyl and carboxylate.

Room temperature optical properties of the obtained CDs. Generally, the fluorescence of CDs will be largely quenched when they are dried into powder due to aggregation [18,25]. One of the most encouraging results of this work is that the obtained CDs preserve luminescence consistency in both aqueous and solid-state. Fig. 4 presents the steady state luminescence spectra for both CDs. The excitation spectrum of the aqueous CDs consists of a broad band with the maximum absorption located at 360 nm, which is due to the n–π* transitions of CO bonds [27,28]. The excitation spectrum of the solid-state CDs is much broader and has stronger absorption around 385 nm (Fig. S2, ESI†). The excitation spectrum of the aqueous and solid CDs matches well with their UV–visible absorption spectrum. The slight broadening and redshifted excitation spectrum of solid CDs may be resulted from the formation of aggregates, leading to the splitting of excited-state energy levels without significant disturbance on emission wavelength [27]. Under their irrespective optimal excitation (360 nm for aqueous CDs vs. 385 nm for the solid CDs), the aqueous CDs and solid CDs both show blue emission (445 nm for aqueous CDs and 450 nm for the solid CDs) and comparable quantum efficiency (21.7% for aqueous CDs and 20.5% for solid CDs). The observed bright blue emission in solid-state CDs indicated that aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching has been effectively conquered in this work. As indicated by the results from FTIR and XPS, the obtained CDs are functionalized by Na ions. The Na ions formed on the surface are positively charged, which has the effect of keeping the CDs out of direct contact and uniformly dispersed, so that the blue emission retains in the solid CDs [40,41]. To further illustrate the role of Na and to confirm our speculation, two control experiments were done and the results were compared: one used citric acid and glycine as starting materials, and the other used the same starting materials but added additional NaOH as the Na source. It can be seen that solid CDs without Na+ ions have a weak blue emission, while that with the addition of NaOH as the Na source have a stronger emission, indicating that surface functionalization of Na+ is helpful to realize solid state luminescence of CDs (Fig. S3, ESI†). In addition, neither the emission maxima of aqueous CDs nor that of the solid CDs change upon increasing excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 385 nm, suggesting both CDs have a single type of emissive state. The excitation independent emission is also one of the characteristics of molecular fluorophores and suggests that the luminescence of both CDs is from a molecular state [27].

For further understanding of the luminescence quenching resistant mechanism of the as-prepared solid CDs, we measured luminescence decay curves for both aqueous CDs and solid CDs (Fig. 5 ). The decay curve of the aqueous state CDs shows a single exponential decay feature with a lifetime of 8.22 ns (Fig. S4, ESI†), implying that there is only one electron−hole recombination process that occurs in these aqueous CDs. In contrast, the decay curve of the solid CDs contains two components with an average lifetime of 9.19 ns (a fast component of 5.0 ns with an amplitude of 65.5% and a slow component of 12.4 ns with an amplitude of 34.5%) (Fig. S5, ESI†). The nonradiative recombination and energy loss induced by the interaction between CDs and water may be reduced in solid CDs, which leads to a slightly longer lifetime in solid CDs [40]. In addition, the slight longer lifetime for the solid CDs also verifies that FRET in the solid-state CDs is suppressed, because FRET competes with radiative transition and shortens the lifetime [42].

Temperature-dependent steady state and transient state luminescence of the aqueous CDs. In order to investigate the effect of temperature on the optical properties and explore the possibility of the obtained aqueous CDs as a nano-thermal probe, the temperature dependent emission spectra of aqueous CDs were measured and shown in Fig. 6 a. It is found that the emission intensity of the aqueous CDs decreases upon increasing the temperature, in specific, the emission intensity decreases about 63% when the temperature is increased from 20 °C to 70 °C, which is caused by the thermally activated population of non-radiative channels of surface (trap/defect) states [32,43,44]. At low temperatures, the non-radiative channels are not thermally activated, so the excited electrons can radiatively emit photons, but as the temperature increases, the non-radiative channels are thermally activated, leading to a decrease in emission [43]. To quantitively evaluate the temperature dependent luminescence properties of the aqueous CDs, Fig. 6b shows the linear regression equation between I/I0 and temperature (T) with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.989 for the regression fit: I/I0 = −0.0126 × T+1.23, where I0 and I are the emission intensity at and above 20 °C, respectively. Based on the slope of the linear fit, the thermal sensitivity was determined to be 1.26%/°C, which is much higher than other previously reported thermal sensors based on the temperature-dependent photoluminescence intensity of CDs, as shown in Table 1 . Upon cooling the temperature, the emission intensity also shows a good linear response to temperature. Owing to the good linear response of I/I0 to temperature, the aqueous CDs may have great application potential as a precise nano-thermometer. The stability of temperature response to CDs was assessed by consecutive heating and cooling cycles over the temperature range of 20–70 °C. As shown in Fig. 6c, after each heating-cooling cycle, the emission intensity can be recovered, indicating the good reversibility and reusability of the aqueous CDs.

In addition to studying the effect of temperature on the steady-state spectrum of aqueous CDs, we also investigated the effect of temperature on the transient spectrum of the aqueous CDs. Fig. 7 a shows the temperature dependent luminescence decay curves of the aqueous CDs, from which it can be seen that the lifetime keeps decreasing from 8.15 ns to 3.01 nsas the temperature increases from 20 °C to 70 °C. The decreased lifetime is a consequence of increased non-radiation upon increasing temperature, since the measured lifetime (τ) is related to the radiative rate constant (kr) and the non-radiative rate constant (knr): τ = 1/(kr + knr). More importantly, all decay curves exhibit mono-exponential decay kinetics over the studied temperature range, indicating that recombination occurs via the single emissive channel [45]. The single exponential decay feature is convenient for thermal sensing because there is only a single lifetime constant for each decay curve and the temperature can be read directly by using calibration. Similar to the emission intensity, the lifetime (τ) of the aqueous CDs responds linearly to temperature (T) as well (Fig. 7b): τ = − 0.1 T + 9.94 (R2 = 0.991). Thermal linearity is advantageous since it makes the correlation between lifetime and temperature simple while providing constant thermal sensitivity over the entire dynamic range [45,46]. The thermal sensitivity of this lifetime based thermal sensor was determined to be 0.1 ns/°C, which is also higher than that (0.08 ns/°C) of the CDs based thermal sensor using temperature dependent photoluminescence lifetime reported in the Ref.[46].

Ionic strength,
pH
stability, photostability and biocompatibility assessment of the aqueous CDs. To explore the practical application of the obtained aqueous CDs for intracellular temperature sensing, we have examined the effect of ionic strength and pH on their emission intensity and lifetime, as well as their cytotoxicity. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the emission intensity of the aqueous CDs has a slight change even in a 1000 mM NaCl solution whose ionic strength is much greater than the typical physiological value of ∼100 mM, demonstrating the excellent ionic stability of the aqueous CDs. Fig. 8b shows the variation in the emission intensity of the aqueous CDs under different pH environments, and it is found that there is no significant change in emission intensity when the pH of the aqueous CDs is varied from 3 to 12, indicating that the aqueous CDs have good stability over a wide pH range. Also, the aqueous CDs show high photostability, as evidenced by the observation that the emission intensity of aqueous CDs remains almost unchanged after 8 h of continuous irradiation with a 6 W 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 8c). Fig. 8d shows the viability of TCA-8113 cells incubated with the aqueous CDs with different concentrations for 24 h. A high cell viability of 80% was observed even with CD concentrations up to 1600 μg/mL, indicating a low cytotoxicity of CDs. Besides the emission intensity, the lifetime of the aqueous CDs is also very stable in different salinity and pH environments as well as in a wide concentration range (0.3 mg/mL – 30 mg/mL) (Figs. S6–S8, ESI†).

Application demonstration of the obtained CDs in living cell and white LEDs. The linear temperature response to intensity and decay time, the excellent pH and ionic stability together with the low cytotoxicity make the aqueous CDs suitable for intracellular temperature measurement. The applicability of the aqueous CDs for intracellular temperature sensing was verified by confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscopy. The confocal laser scanning microscopic images of tongue squamous cell carcinoma cells (TCA-8113 cells) incubated with CDs (600 μg/mL) in bright field mode and fluorescent mode at 20 °C is shown in Fig. 9 a and b, respectively. It can be seen that the TCA-8113 cells adhered to the cell culture dish and maintained the normal morphology of the living cells (Fig. 9a), and the TCA-8113 cells exhibited bright blue luminescence under 405 nm excitation (Fig. 9b), showing the CDs can be used as a potential biomarker. Differing from the bright blue luminescence, no visible luminescence can be detected in control cells (without incubation with CDs) under the same condition (Fig. S9, ESI†), indicating CDs mainly stayed in the cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area. More importantly, the blue emission from CDs incubated in TCA-8113 cells became weaker when the temperature was increased from 20 °C to 37 °C (Fig. 9c), and recovered when the temperature was cooled to 20 °C again (Fig. S10, ESI†), well illustrating the good applicability of the aqueous CDs for intracellular temperature sensing.

Because of their strong solid-state fluorescence and high photostability under UV light irradiation (Fig. S11, ESI†), the solid CDs were used to fabricate prototype white LEDs. Shown in Fig. 10 a is the emission spectrum of the prototype white LED operating at 20 mA. The insets are the images of the fabricated white LEDs lamp that works at the same bias current. The emission spectrum consists of four distinctive bands with peaks at 390 nm, 445 nm, 525 nm and 615 nm, which are derived from the superimposed emission of the n-UV chip, solid CDs, and green emitting phosphor (Ba, Sr)2SiO4: Eu2+ and red emitting phosphor CaAlSiN3:Eu2+, respectively. The Commission International de l’Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates, correlated color temperature (CCT), and color rendering index (CRI) of the generated white light were found to be (0.325, 0.355), 5805 K, and 96, respectively. The excellent CRI of the prototype white LEDs is attributed to its broad-band emission feature. To analyze the optical stability of the solid CDs based white LEDs, the EL spectra of the fabricated white LEDs at different driving currents are measured and shown in Fig. 10b, from which it can be seen that the spectral intensity increased with the driving current. The CCT, CRI, luminescence efficiency (η) and color coordinates at different operating currents are summarized in Table 2 . As the drive current increased from 20 to 100 mA, the CCT, luminescence efficiency and color coordinates vary from 5805 K, 40.4 lm/W and (0.325, 0.355) to 5409 K, 48.9 lm/W and (0.335, 0.375). These variations upon increasing driving current mainly are attributed to higher thermal quenching of solid CDs than that of CaAlSiN3:Eu2+ and (Ba,Sr)2SiO4:Eu2+, which can be witnessed from the normalized EL spectra in the inset of Fig. 10b and the temperature dependent emission spectra of the solid CDs (Fig. S12, ESI†). The above results demonstrate that the obtained solid CDs may serve as a promising blue color converter for low power white LEDs.

In summary, CDs possessing bright blue emissions in an aqueous state and solid state have been successfully prepared. The surface functionalization of sodium carboxylate can suppress the fluorescence resonance energy transfer among CDs so that CDs retain blue emission even after drying into powder. More importantly, the emission intensity and lifetime of the aqueous CDs are linearly responsive to temperature over the physiological temperature range of 20–70 °C and are stable at different pH values (3–12), salinity environments (0.1–1 mol/L NaCl solution) and concentrations (0.3 mg/mL-30 mg/mL), making them suitable for probing intracellular temperature. The thermal sensitivity of CDs-based nanothermometers derived from the linear relationship between temperature and photoluminescence intensity/lifetime is 1.26%/°C and 0.1 ns/°C, respectively, which is better than the previously reported CDs-based nanothermometers. The cell experiments confirm that the aqueous CDs have good biocompatibility and potential applicability for intracellular temperature sensing. A prototype white LEDs device with a correlated color temperature of 5805 K, a color rendering index of 96 and luminescence efficiency of 40.4 lm/W under 20 mA driven current has been demonstrated by using the blue emitting solid CD as a color converter.
Junlu Guo: Investigation, Formal analysis; Liying Qin: Investigation, Formal analysis; Deyin Wang: Supervising, Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 51602137, U1905213), Lanzhou Chengguan Science and Technology Bureau (grant no. 2018KJGG0097), Science and Technology Department of Gansu Province (grant no. 20JR10RA624).
